title: Difference of squares intro
description: Factor quadratic expressions into the special products of the general form (x+a)(x-a).
topic_path: Get ready for limits and continuity
Factoring quadratics with difference of squares

assessment_items.question_content:
**Factor as the product of two binomials.**

$x^2-16=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
Both $x^2$ and $16$ are *perfect squares,* since $x^2=(\blueD{x})^2$ and $16=(\greenD{4})^2$.

$x^2-16 = (\blueD{x})^2-(\greenD{4})^2$

So we can use the *difference of squares* pattern to factor.

$\blueD{a}^2 - \greenD{b}^2 =(\blueD{a}+\greenD{b})(\blueD{a}-\greenD{b})$

In this case, $\blueD{a}=\blueD{x}$ and $\greenD{b}=\greenD{4}$:

$(\blueD{x})^2 - (\greenD{4})^2 =(\blueD{x}+\greenD{4})(\blueD{x}-\greenD{4})$

In conclusion,

$x^2-16=(x+4)(x-4)$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Factor as the product of two binomials.**

$25-x^2=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
Both $25$ and $x^2$ are *perfect squares,* since $25=(\blueD{5})^2$ and $x^2=(\greenD{x})^2$.

$25-x^2 = (\blueD{5})^2-(\greenD{x})^2$

So we can use the *difference of squares* pattern to factor.

$\blueD{a}^2 - \greenD{b}^2 =(\blueD{a}+\greenD{b})(\blueD{a}-\greenD{b})$

In this case, $\blueD{a}=\blueD{5}$ and $\greenD{b}=\greenD{a}$:

$(\blueD{5})^2 - (\greenD{x})^2 =(\blueD{5}+\greenD{x})(\blueD{5}-\greenD{x})$

In conclusion,

$25-x^2=(5+x)(5-x)$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

assessment_items.question_content:
The rectangle below has an area of $x^2-25$ square meters and a length of $x+5$ meters. 

**What expression represents the width of the rectangle?**



[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Width} = $ [[☃ expression 1]] meters
hints:
Both $x^2$ and $25$ are *perfect squares,* since $x^2=(\blueD{x})^2$ and $25=(\greenD{5})^2$.

$x^2-25 = (\blueD{x})^2-(\greenD{5})^2$

So we can use the *difference of squares* pattern to factor.

$\blueD{a}^2 - \greenD{b}^2 =(\blueD{a}+\greenD{b})(\blueD{a}-\greenD{b})$

In this case, $\blueD{a}=\blueD{x}$ and $\greenD{b}=\greenD{5}$:

$(\blueD{x})^2 - (\greenD{5})^2 =(\blueD{x}+\greenD{5})(\blueD{x}-\greenD{5})$

In conclusion,

$x^2-25=(x+5)(x-5)$

Since $\text{Length} = x+5$ meters, we can conclude that $\text{Width} = x-5$ meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

options.alt: A rectangle with an unknown width and a length of x plus five. The area of the rectangle is x squared minus twenty-five.

assessment_items.question_content:
The rectangle below has an area of $121-x^2$ square meters and a length of $11-x$ meters. 

**What expression represents the width of the rectangle?**



[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Width} = $ [[☃ expression 1]] meters
hints:
Both $121$ and $x^2$ are *perfect squares,* since $121=(\blueD{11})^2$ and $x^2=(\greenD{x})^2$.

$121-x^2= (\blueD{11})^2-(\greenD{x})^2$

So we can use the *difference of squares* pattern to factor.

$\blueD{a}^2 - \greenD{b}^2 =(\blueD{a}+\greenD{b})(\blueD{a}-\greenD{b})$

In this case, $\blueD{a}=\blueD{11}$ and $\greenD{b}=\greenD{x}$:

$(\blueD{11})^2 - (\greenD{x})^2 =(\blueD{11}+\greenD{x})(\blueD{11}-\greenD{x})$

In conclusion,

$121-x^2=(11+x)(11-x)$

Since $\text{Length} = 11-x$ meters, we can conclude that $\text{Width} = 11+x$ meters.



[[☃ image 1]]

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

options.alt: A rectangle with an unknown width and a length of eleven minus x. The area of the rectangle is one hundred twenty-one minus x squared.
